mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the feature extraction stage in pattern classification?

options.text: To assign a class label to an unknown object.
To reduce noise and isolate objects from the background.
To calculate descriptive and discriminative numerical values from a segmented object.
To define the decision boundaries between classes in the feature space.

mcqs.explanation: Feature extraction is the process of computing measurable properties (features) from the pre-processed and segmented image. These features form the pattern vector used for classification. Option A describes the classifier's job, Option B describes pre-processing/segmentation, and Option D is a result of the classifier's design.

mcqs.question: In the Minimum Distance Classifier, how is the prototype for a class typically defined?

options.text: The pattern farthest from all other class members.
The median value of all features in the class.
The mean vector (centroid) of all training patterns in the class.
The pattern that is closest to the decision boundary.

mcqs.explanation: The most common and simple prototype used in the MDC is the mean vector (average of all feature vectors) for the patterns in a given class. This represents the central point or centroid of the class in feature space.

mcqs.question: An unknown pattern x is to be classified. The Euclidean distances from x to the prototypes of classes A, B, and C are 5.2, 3.1, and 7.8, respectively. Using the Minimum Distance Classifier, which class will x be assigned to?

options.text: Class A
Class B
Class C
It cannot be determined without the actual feature vectors.

mcqs.explanation: The core rule of the MDC is to assign the pattern to the class whose prototype is closest (has the smallest distance). Here, the smallest distance is 3.1 to Class B.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a significant limitation of the basic Minimum Distance Classifier using Euclidean distance?

options.text: It requires a large amount of memory to store all training samples.
It assumes that all classes form spherical clusters of approximately the same size in feature space.
Its decision boundaries are highly complex and non-linear.
It cannot be used for classification tasks with more than two classes.

mcqs.explanation: The Euclidean MDC inherently assumes that the natural grouping for a class is spherical because a circle (or hypersphere) defines a set of points equidistant from a center. It performs poorly if a class has an elongated or irregular shape, or if classes have vastly different variances.

mcqs.question: How are the decision boundaries formed in a Minimum Distance Classifier for a multi-class problem?

options.text: They are complex neural networks.
They are perpendicular bisectors of the lines joining pairs of prototype points, forming Voronoi cells.
They are based on the probability density functions of each class.
They are parallel to the feature axes.

mcqs.explanation: The decision boundary between any two classes is defined by the set of points that are equidistant to the two class prototypes. This boundary is a hyperplane that is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment connecting the two prototypes. The collection of these boundaries partitions the space into Voronoi cells.
